skill.name: to-prd
skill.description: Turn the current conversation context into a PRD and publish it to the project issue tracker. Use when user wants to create a PRD from the current context.
skill.summary: Convert conversation context into a structured Product Requirements Document and publish it to the project issue tracker.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Ruby/shell backtick execution and weak cryptographic algorithms. These are false positives. The backticks on lines 8 and 20 are markdown inline code syntax (not Ruby execution), and the weak crypto flags appear to be triggered by unrelated pattern keywords. The skill is an instructional document for generating PRDs with no execution risk, network access, or data exfiltration patterns.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Syntax Misidentified as Ruby Backtick Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Lines 8 and 20 contain markdown inline code using backticks (e.g., `/setup-matt-pocock-skills` and `needs-triage`). These are markdown formatting, not Ruby/shell backtick execution. No command injection risk exists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are clearly within markdown text blocks, not code execution contexts. The skill is a markdown documentation file with no scripting functionality.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Lines 3, 62, and 68 triggered 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags. These lines contain ordinary PRD documentation text with no cryptographic operations. The static scanner appears to have flagged unrelated pattern keywords.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic algorithms, encoding, or hashing operations exist in this documentation-only skill. The static scanner's pattern matching incorrectly flagged common English words.

content.value_statement: Writing PRDs manually takes hours of back-and-forth with stakeholders. This skill synthesizes your existing conversation context into a structured, publication-ready PRD and publishes it directly to your issue tracker.
actual_capabilities: Analyze existing codebase structure and domain glossary
Generate comprehensive PRD documents from conversation context
Apply triage labels to published issues
Suggest modular architecture with testable deep modules
Produce detailed user stories in standard format
limitations: Requires `/setup-matt-pocock-skills` to be run first for issue tracker configuration
Does not interview users or ask clarifying questions
Cannot access private repositories without proper authentication

use_cases.title: Feature Kickoff Documentation
use_cases.description: When a stakeholder describes a feature in a meeting, use this skill to immediately generate a structured PRD without additional interviews.

use_cases.title: Sprint Planning Support
use_cases.description: Quickly convert verbal or chat-based requirements into formal PRDs with implementation decisions and testing plans.

use_cases.title: Technical Writing Automation
use_cases.description: Generate standardized PRD documents using a consistent template across your entire engineering team.

prompt_templates.title: Basic PRD Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the to-prd skill to create a PRD from our conversation about [feature name].
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new feature that was just discussed

prompt_templates.title: PRD with Architecture Focus
prompt_templates.prompt: Use to-prd to generate a PRD. Focus particularly on identifying deep modules that can be tested in isolation.

prompt_templates.title: Minimal PRD for Small Features
prompt_templates.prompt: Use to-prd for this quick feature request. Keep the user stories concise but complete.

prompt_templates.title: PRD with Testing Emphasis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use to-prd to create a PRD. Include extensive testing decisions and prior art from existing tests.

output_examples.input: User describes wanting to add file upload capability with progress tracking and retry logic
output_examples.output: A PRD document with Problem Statement, Solution, 10+ user stories, Implementation Decisions covering module boundaries, Testing Decisions referencing existing upload tests, and Out of Scope items

output_examples.input: Stakeholder wants a notification system with email and SMS support
output_examples.output: PRD with user stories for each notification channel, module design for NotificationService abstraction, testing strategy mentioning existing notification test patterns

output_examples.input: Team needs a dashboard with real-time data visualization
output_examples.output: PRD covering data fetching modules, visualization components, testing approaches for async updates, and explicit out-of-scope items like mobile support

best_practices: Run `/setup-matt-pocock-skills` once before using this skill for the first time
Have existing ADRs and domain glossary available for the AI to reference
Review module suggestions and provide feedback before the PRD is finalized
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill without running setup first
Do not expect the AI to ask clarifying questions - it synthesizes from existing context
Do not request file paths or code snippets in PRDs

faq.question: What happens if I have not run `/setup-matt-pocock-skills`?
faq.answer: The skill will prompt you to run the setup command first to configure your issue tracker.

faq.question: Can I edit the PRD after it is published?
faq.answer: Yes, PRDs are regular issues that can be updated like any other issue in your tracker.

faq.question: Does this skill ask me questions during PRD generation?
faq.answer: No, it synthesizes what it already knows from the conversation context without additional interviews.

faq.question: What triage label is applied to the PRD?
faq.answer: The `needs-triage` label is applied so the PRD enters the normal triage workflow.

faq.question: Can I use this for urgent hotfixes?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for structured feature development, not emergency hotfixes which typically bypass the PRD process.

faq.question: What format are the user stories in?
faq.answer: User stories follow the format: As a [actor], I want [feature], so that [benefit].
